howItWorks.intro: Proper diesel tank sizing ensures you never run out of fuel while avoiding an oversized, expensive tank. The calculation accounts for daily consumption, delivery schedules, and an emergency reserve buffer.
rules: Minimum capacity = daily usage × (delivery frequency + reserve days)
Standard above-ground tank sizes: 250, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 gallons
The recommended tank is the smallest standard size that meets or exceeds your minimum requirement
howItWorks.outro: Tanks should generally be filled when they reach 20-25% capacity to avoid condensation and sediment issues. Your actual usable capacity is about 90-95% of the rated tank size due to expansion space requirements.

faq.question: What standard diesel tank sizes are available?
faq.answer: Common above-ground storage tank sizes are 250, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 gallons. Smaller tanks (100-150 gallon) are available for single-vehicle operations, and larger tanks (5,000-10,000+ gallon) for large fleet operations. Underground tanks are also available but require additional permits.

faq.question: How many reserve days should I plan for?
faq.answer: 3-5 reserve days is standard for most operations. If you're in a remote area or during winter when deliveries may be delayed, plan for 5-7 days. Operations that cannot tolerate downtime (hospitals, data centers) may keep 7-14 days of reserve.

faq.question: Do I need a permit for a diesel storage tank?
faq.answer: Requirements vary by location and tank size. Generally, tanks under 1,100 gallons on a farm or construction site have fewer requirements. Tanks over 1,320 gallons total capacity on site typically require a Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) plan under EPA regulations.

faq.question: How much does a diesel storage tank cost?
faq.answer: A 250-gallon above-ground tank costs $800-1,500. A 500-gallon tank runs $1,200-2,500. A 1,000-gallon tank costs $2,000-4,000. A 2,000-gallon tank runs $4,000-8,000. Prices include the tank only — pumps, containment, and installation are additional.

faq.question: Should I go with a larger tank than calculated?
faq.answer: Going one size up is often smart. A larger tank lets you buy fuel in bigger quantities (usually at a lower per-gallon price), provides more buffer for delivery delays, and accommodates business growth. The cost difference between tank sizes is modest compared to the convenience.

faq.question: How often should I have diesel delivered?
faq.answer: Most operations schedule deliveries every 7-14 days. More frequent deliveries mean a smaller tank but higher delivery costs and more logistical overhead. Less frequent deliveries need a larger tank but often get better per-gallon pricing on larger drops.
